query: Percentage of Phosphoric Acid Reaction Yielding Dicalcium Phosphate Monohydrate

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Several methods exist for producing hydroxyapatite from synthetic or natural sources. Synthetic hydroxyapatite is derived from raw materials such as calcium carbonate, calcium hydroxide, calcium nitrate, diammonium hydrogen phosphate, and ammonium hydroxide. The fabrication process of hydroxyapatite involves both wet methods and solid-state reactions, followed by calcination or sintering. These methods utilize chemical reactions that adjust the content of CaO and TCP to achieve the stoichiometric conditions necessary for hydroxyapatite formation.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyapatite is an inorganic mineral with a typical apatite lattice structure represented as (A10(BO4)6C2), where A, B, and C correspond to Ca, PO 4 , and OH, respectively. Pure hydroxyapatite contains 39.68% calcium and 18% phosphorus by weight, resulting in a Ca/P molar ratio of 1.67. Commercial hydroxyapatite products have Ca/P ratios that can be higher or lower than 1.67. This variation in the Ca/P ratio indicates a phase shift between tricalcium phosphate (TCP) and calcium oxide (CaO). Hydroxyapatite with a Ca/P ratio greater than 1.67 contains more CaO than TCP, while those with a lower ratio contain more TCP. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: In contrast, the solid-state reaction yields a stoichiometric and well-defined crystalline structure of hydroxyapatite. However, this method requires high temperatures and extended heat treatment procedures. The raw materials used in the solid-state method should have a Ca/P ratio of 1.67 and must be ball-milled to ensure uniform particle size. [2] The solid-state reaction method solely depends on the solid diffusion of ions into the raw materials, thereby necessitating temperatures around 1250 ºC to initiate the reaction. [2] Moreover, prolonged heat treatment transforms singular crystalline particles into more blocky crystals, and the increase in crystalline size leads to a decrease in porosity, which is associated with the aging process. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The wet method produces nonstoichiometric hydroxyapatite powder that may contain impurities such as hydrogen phosphate, carbonate, chloride, and sodium ions. These impurities can lead to the formation of calcium-deficient hydroxyapatite. [1] Previous studies have identified these impurities as uncontrollable variables that can significantly alter the crystallographic arrangement and chemical properties of hydroxyapatite, ultimately affecting its dissolution process.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperphosphatemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The most common measurement of serum inorganic phosphate is the reaction of phosphate ions with ammonium molybdate, in acidic pH,  to form a colorless phosphomolybdate complex that can be measured directly by ultraviolet (UV) absorption at 340 nm, or after reduction to colored molybdenum blue at 600 to 700 nm. Currently, no reference method exists for the measurement of serum phosphate.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperphosphatemia -- Treatment / Management -- Magnesium carbonate
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium carbonate effectively reduces serum phosphate levels and shows good gastrointestinal tolerance. It also reduces vascular calcification by interfering with hydroxyapatite formation.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanical properties of hydroxyapatite are influenced by factors such as phase composition, crystal size, and synthesis method. Pure hydroxyapatite typically has a bending strength of 38 to 250 MPa, compressive strength of 120 to 150 MPa, and tensile strength of 38 to 300 MPa. The Young modulus ranges from 35 to 120 GPa, depending on the presence of impurities. [19] Meanwhile, the Weibull modulus, ranging from 5 to 18, indicates hydroxyapatite is a brittle material. To enhance its mechanical properties, tough hydroxyapatite can be obtained by incorporating TCP, which increases its flexural strength. In contrast, the flexural strength reaches its minimum when hydroxyapatite contains calcium oxide CaO. [10] [19] Furthermore, the sintering temperature also significantly alters the mechanical properties of hydroxyapatite. Higher sintering temperatures lead to increased density, compressive strength, grain size, and torsional strength. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperphosphatemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Repeat phosphate estimation should take place to confirm hyperphosphatemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Phosphate -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the skeleton, the majority of the phosphate is in combination with calcium in the form of hydroxyapatite crystals, and the rest is present in the form of amorphous calcium phosphate. There are two forms of phosphate present in the serum, dihydrogen phosphate( H2PO4) and mono hydrogen phosphate (HPO4); the balance between these two forms depends on the acid-base status of the body . [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Several challenges exist regarding the application of hydroxyapatite in medicine. For instance, using hydroxyapatite as an implant presents inherent defects and fine porosity that can act as crack initiators. Following crack initiation, propagation can lead to catastrophic failure during use. Additionally, the application of bulk hydroxyapatite may result in a modulus mismatch between the bone and the implant, leading to disproportionate load sharing. [10] Conversely, hydroxyapatite, irrespective of its source, always contains trace elements such as fluoride ions (F - ) and hydroxyl ions (OH - ), which contribute to increased crystallite size and decreased solubility, thereby enhancing the strength of the apatite. [19] At the same time, elements such as phosphide ions (PO 3 3- ) and chloride ions (Cl - ) have been shown to weaken the mechanical properties of hydroxyapatite by reducing crystallite size and increasing solubility. [10] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyapatite from natural sources is commonly fabricated from materials such as fishbone, coral, bovine bone, eggshell, and seashells through a calcination process. Hydroxyapatite produced from these natural sources is often nonstoichiometric due to the presence of trace ions. [13] [17] These traces of ions, which include cations such as Na + , K + , Mg 2+ , Sr 2+ , Zn 2+ , and Al 3+ , as well as anions such as F - , Cl - , SO 4 2- , and CO 3 2- , are beneficial for promoting rapid bone regeneration. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Reconstructing damaged hard tissue is essential for various reasons, including traumatic or nontraumatic events, congenital abnormalities, and disease. Such damage can significantly impact orthopedic, dental, and maxillofacial surgeries. Research on numerous biomaterials shows that calcium phosphates have been utilized in hard tissue reconstruction for over 6 decades, with hydroxyapatite being the primary material used in orthopedics and dentistry.

retrieved_docs.title: Antacids -- Administration -- Hyperphosphatemia
retrieved_docs.content: The total dose is not to exceed 2000 mg per day. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperphosphatemia -- Treatment / Management -- Phosphate Binders
retrieved_docs.content: Aluminum-based agents are amongst the most effective and best tolerated. But doubts regarding their potential to cause aluminum toxicity, presenting with encephalopathy, osteomalacia, microcytic anemia, and premature death, have discouraged their prolonged use.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Phosphate -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Of the total phosphate in the body, 85% is n the bones and teeth,1% in the extracellular fluid and the remaining 14% is distributed in other tissues where it is an important constituent of cell membranes, nucleic acids, high energy phosphate esters (ATP) and intracellular signaling proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium Pyrophosphate Deposition Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Previous studies have demonstrated the usefulness of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), particularly gradient-echo sequences, for the evaluation of calcium pyrophosphate crystal deposition burden in joint cartilage. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1916
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium acetate and calcium phosphate dibasic are both classified as calcium compounds. As members of the broader calcium compounds category, they share the fundamental property of containing calcium ions, yet they are applied differently in medical practice. Calcium acetate is frequently used as a phosphate binder for patients with chronic kidney disease, helping to control serum phosphate levels, while calcium phosphate dibasic serves as a calcium source in nutritional supplements and supports bone health. Recognizing that both agents belong to the calcium compounds group aids clinicians in understanding their chemical characteristics and therapeutic roles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1306
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium Compounds represent a class of calcium‑containing chemical substances that include several distinct calcium salts. Among these are calcium glycerophosphate, which is a calcium‑based glycerophosphate compound, calcium acetate, a calcium salt of acetic acid, and tricalcium phosphate, a calcium phosphate used in various medical and nutritional contexts. All three entities share calcium as a central component, linking them together as members of the broader category of calcium compounds that are relevant for applications such as phosphate binding, calcium supplementation, and bone health management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2449
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium phosphate is classified among the drugs used for treatment of hypercalcemia, indicating its role in managing elevated calcium levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2290
retrieved_docs.content: Bulk compounding chemicals are a class of substances used in pharmaceutical preparation, and within this class calcium oxide, calcium phosphate dibasic, and tricalcium phosphate are identified as specific members. Calcium compounds represent another category of calcium‑based substances, comprising calcium acetate, calcium oxide, calcium phosphate dibasic, and tricalcium phosphate. Consequently, calcium oxide, calcium phosphate dibasic, and tricalcium phosphate are recognized both as bulk compounding chemicals and as calcium compounds, highlighting their dual role in formulation processes and calcium chemistry. This overlap underscores the importance of these agents in both compounding practices and calcium‑related therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2778
retrieved_docs.content: Replacement preparation is classified as a sodium glycerophosphate formulation and it also includes dibasic potassium phosphate as a subordinate component.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: Replacement preparation includes dibasic potassium phosphate and sodium hyaluronate as its constituent components, indicating that both dibasic potassium phosphate and sodium hyaluronate are child elements of the replacement preparation formulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1918
retrieved_docs.content: Replacement preparation is a medical formulation that incorporates several specific components, including kalcium‑glycerolfosfát, dibasic potassium phosphate, and sodium hyaluronate. Kalcium‑glycerolfosfát contributes calcium and phosphate ions that can support mineral balance, while dibasic potassium phosphate provides additional phosphate and potassium ions to help maintain physiological pH and electrolyte homeostasis. Sodium hyaluronate, a high‑molecular‑weight glycosaminoglycan, offers viscoelastic properties that are valuable for joint lubrication and tissue repair. Together, these ingredients define the composition of the replacement preparation, highlighting its role in delivering essential minerals and lubricating agents for therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1915
retrieved_docs.content: Bulk compounding chemicals refer to the inorganic substances used in large‑scale pharmaceutical preparation, and this category includes both calcium oxide and tricalcium phosphate. Calcium oxide is a basic oxide that can be employed to adjust pH or provide calcium ions in compounded formulations. Tricalcium phosphate, on the other hand, serves as a calcium and phosphate source and is commonly used as a filler or binder in oral dosage forms. By recognizing calcium oxide and tricalcium phosphate as members of the bulk compounding chemicals group, formulators can select appropriate compounds to achieve desired physicochemical properties and therapeutic outcomes in compounded medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2103
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium acetate is classified as a calcium compound, reflecting an “isa” relationship that places calcium acetate within the broader category of calcium compounds. This categorization indicates that calcium acetate shares the chemical characteristics of calcium compounds and informs its use in medical applications, such as serving as a phosphate binder in patients with renal impairment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium acetate and tricalcium phosphate are both classified as calcium compounds, indicating that each belongs to the broader category of calcium‑containing substances. As members of this class, they share the fundamental chemical characteristic of providing bioavailable calcium and interacting with phosphate metabolism. Recognizing calcium acetate and tricalcium phosphate as calcium compounds helps clarify their roles in medical nutrition, calcium supplementation, and the management of bone health and phosphate balance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1896
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium oxide and calcium phosphate dibasic are both classified as bulk compounding chemicals, indicating that they are used in large‑scale preparation of pharmaceutical formulations. As bulk compounding chemicals, these calcium‑based compounds serve as essential excipients or active ingredients in the compounding process, providing sources of calcium and contributing to the buffering capacity, stability, and bioavailability of compounded medications. Their role as bulk compounding chemicals underscores their importance in manufacturing sterile and non‑sterile preparations where precise dosing and consistent quality are critical for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium compounds constitute a class of chemical substances that contain calcium, and this class includes calcium glycerophosphate, calcium acetate, and calcium oxide. Calcium glycerophosphate is a calcium‑phosphate salt that can serve as a source of both minerals. Calcium acetate is commonly employed as a phosphate‑binding agent in patients with renal failure to reduce serum phosphate levels. Calcium oxide, also known as quicklime, can be used as a calcium supplement or antacid in various medical contexts. All three substances are categorized under the broader term “Calcium Compounds,” reflecting their shared elemental composition while each possesses distinct chemical properties and clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1442
retrieved_docs.content: Bone meal, calcium acetate, and calcium oxide are all classified as calcium compounds. As members of the calcium compounds category, each of these substances contains calcium and can be employed in medical practice for calcium‑related therapeutic purposes. Calcium acetate is commonly used as a phosphate binder in patients with chronic kidney disease, helping to control serum phosphate levels. Calcium oxide serves as a source of elemental calcium that can be administered as a supplement or incorporated into topical preparations to support bone health and calcium balance. Bone meal provides both calcium and phosphorus, offering a natural source of these minerals that can aid in bone remodeling and nutritional supplementation. Recognizing that bone meal, calcium acetate, and calcium oxide belong to the broader group of calcium compounds assists clinicians in selecting appropriate calcium‑based interventions for various patient care needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2160
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium compounds encompass several specific calcium salts, including kalcium‑glycerolfosfát, calcium acetate, and calcium phosphate dibasic. Each of these entities is classified as a type of calcium compound, sharing the fundamental chemical characteristic of containing calcium ions bound to various anionic groups. Kalcium‑glycerolfosfát represents a calcium‑glycerol‑phosphate complex, calcium acetate is the calcium salt of acetic acid, and calcium phosphate dibasic is a calcium salt of phosphoric acid. Recognizing these substances as members of the broader calcium compound category highlights their related pharmacologic and nutritional roles, such as providing calcium supplementation, acting as phosphate binders, or serving as buffering agents in clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1482
retrieved_docs.content: Bulk compounding chemicals are identified as calcium phosphate dibasic and are also classified as tricalcium phosphate, indicating that these bulk substances belong to both chemical categories and serve as sources of calcium and phosphate in pharmaceutical compounding formulations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2788
retrieved_docs.content: Replacement preparation is classified as a potassium supplement and includes calcium lactate and dibasic potassium phosphate as its constituent components, indicating that this formulation belongs to the broader category of potassium supplements while being composed of those specific ingredients.
